title: Why Do Entire Industries Do the Wrong Thing Together?

challenges.stem: Your engineering team shares an on-call rotation. One engineer consistently does the minimum during their shifts — responding slowly, leaving partial notes, pushing issues to the next person. Everyone notices. Nobody confronts it. Why?

options.text: Diffusion of responsibility — in a shared rotation, no single person feels accountable for confronting the free-rider, and each person hopes someone else will handle it
Fear of conflict — engineers are non-confrontational and avoid difficult conversations
The free-rider is politically protected — people sense that confronting them would backfire
The team lacks clear standards for on-call quality

challenges.explanation: This is the bystander effect in a professional context — diffusion of responsibility. In a group, each person feels 1/N responsible for confronting the issue. Fear (B), politics (C), and standards (D) might contribute, but the primary mechanism is that nobody feels it's specifically THEIR job to act. The fix: assign clear ownership of on-call quality (shrink the responsible group to one person) or make performance visible (dashboards).

challenges.stem: The Montreal Protocol (ozone) succeeded while climate agreements fail — despite both being environmental collective action problems. What structural difference made ozone solvable?

options.text: Incentives — CFC alternatives were cheap and concentrated in one industry, making cooperation's cost low enough that free-riding incentives were weak
Fear — the 'hole in the ozone' imagery scared people more than gradual warming
Trust — 1987 had more international cooperation and less geopolitical tension
Power — the US and Europe had more political will for environmental action in the 1980s

challenges.explanation: The Montreal Protocol succeeded because structural incentives aligned: alternatives existed (cheap to cooperate), costs were concentrated in one industry (few players with disproportionate impact), and consequences were near-term. Climate fails because cooperation is expensive (entire economy must change), costs are diffuse (every industry), and consequences are distant. It's not about fear, trust, or will — it's about game structure.

pairs.back: Everyone benefits from cooperation, but nobody has individual incentive to cooperate first — because free-riding on others' cooperation is cheaper
Enjoying the benefits of others' cooperation without contributing yourself. Rational for individuals, catastrophic for groups.
In large groups, each person feels 1/N responsible. At large N, nobody feels responsible enough to act. Cooperation never starts.
Most people will cooperate IF they see others cooperating. The deadlock is everyone waiting for everyone else to go first.

pairs.left: Climate change (195 countries, decades away, whole-economy costs)
pairs.right: Structurally unsolvable through voluntary cooperation alone — too many players, too diffuse, too long-term

pairs.left: Montreal Protocol (few CFC producers, cheap alternative, visible hole)
pairs.right: Structurally solvable — concentrated costs, cheap cooperation, visible evidence, near-term stakes

pairs.left: Making team contributions visible on a shared dashboard
pairs.right: Breaks free-riding by removing anonymity — conditional cooperators activate when they see others contributing

pairs.left: Breaking a 50-person shared-resource problem into five 10-person groups
pairs.right: Shrinking the group so social monitoring and reputation effects function

pairs.left: One person documenting code while everyone else benefits without contributing
pairs.right: Classic free-rider problem — the contributor bears costs while benefits are shared equally

before.label: Appealing to conscience
before.text: If we all just did the right thing — contributed fairly, stopped free-riding, cooperated — the problem would be solved. People need to be more responsible.

after.label: Redesigning the game
after.text: Moral appeal alone fails in collective action problems. I make contributions visible, reduce cooperation costs, shrink the group to where monitoring works, and go first to break the 'waiting for others' deadlock. I fix the structure, not the people.
